PSC.question: what is difference between PORT STATE, FLAG STATE & COASTAL STATE?
PSC.answer:
Port State - State where the foreign flagged vessels Calls.

Coastal State - State whose water the ships passes without calling its port.

Flag State - State whose flag the Ship is entitled to fly.

ESP CODE.question: ESP close-up survey & Substantial Corrosion?
ESP CODE.answer:
Close-up survey is a survey where the details of structural components are within the close visual inspection range of the surveyor that is normally within reach of hand. SUBSTANTIAL CORROSION - is an extent of corrosion such that assessment of corrosion pattern indicates wastage in excess of 75% of allowable margins, but within acceptable limits. for example - suppose a new vessel is being build, and classification society says that minimum 20mm thick plate should be there. and i want to run my vessel for minimum 20 years, so i will keep the thickness a bit extra, that is let it be 5mm which will be the safety margin or called as allowable margin. Now if corrosion occurs and allowable margin is corroded more than 75%, means if the corrosion goes above 75% it is called substantial corrosion.


one more example - suspected area where substantial corrosion happens. when a plate is checked for suspected area and substantial corrosion, having allowable margin. onboard we have two files - ship construction file (scf) and ctf (coating thickness file). these files will tell you what the allowable margin for that plate is.  suppose allowable margin is 5mm, so 75% of this allowable margin if corroded, then it has entered substantial corrosion.  


now if you are sailing and substantial corrosion is found by surveyor, and drydock is after 9 months, the first action is renew the plate, but cant be done in sea , so temporary actions can be taken.

1. Hard Epoxy Paint
2. Putty repair can be done
3. DD renew the plate.

APPLICABLE TO - OIL TANKERS & BULK CARRIERS of 500 gt and above.

ESP CODE.question: ESP in detail?
What is ESP?
ESP CODE.answer:
ESP - International Code for the Enhanced Programme of Inspections during Surveys of Bulk Carriers and Oil Tankers. ESP is mentioned in SOLAS Chapter XI-1, Regulation -2. ESP CODE was adopted in 2011and came into force in 2014. Before ESP CODE, resolution - A.744(18) was there which laid GUIDELINES on ENHANCED PROGRAMME, adopted on 4th November 1993, after which in 2011, resolution - a.1049(27) was passed and these guidelines were made mandatory which is now mentioned in SOLAS Chapter XI-1, Regulation 2 - ENHANCED SURVEYS.


ESP CODE applies to Bulk Carriers and Oil Tankers of 500 GT and Above.

ESP CODE.question: Why ESP CODE came for BULK CARRIERS and for OIL TANKERS?
ESP CODE.answer:
WHY FOR BULK CARRIERS - in 1980's a lot of bulk carriers got sanked & were Lost, the major reason were : they were single skin,above 20000 DWT, were having structural failure, average age of bulk carriers were 18 years, heavy cargo being carried, bad weather, water ingress in Cargo Holds. two of the famous accident example are DERBY SHIRE and BULK JUPITER. the main reason was structural failure.  suppose the ship sank, who will pay the money? - insurance company. so the insurance companies were losing money and they pressurized classification society. Because as per classification society - solas chapter ii-1 and regulation 3-1 states that every ship should be designed constructed and maintained as per the rules of the classification society. so when lot of bulk carriers were lost they got money from insurance companies. so IACS came into existence - international association of classification societies, which brought good surveys of bulk carriers and oil, chemical tankers.                                                                                  WHY IT CAME FOR OIL TANKERS - in mid 1980's 1st generation(ALASKAN FLEET) VLCC's, had problem of unchecked corrosion in ballast tanks requiring steel renewal by 2nd renewal inspection (after 10 years).   Following a spate accidents in bulk carriers & tankers, IACS introduced Enhanced survey program for more close up inspections of these ships in 1993.

However, following more accidents, IMO adopted a new SOLAS chapter (Chapter XI-1) to enhance maritime safety on 04 November 1993. ESP is mandatory as per SOLAS Regulation 2 of Chapter XI-1.

The new chapter required bulk carriers and oil tankers to go through enhanced survey program.

on 30 November 2011, new IMO resolution (replacing the earlier one) introduced ESP Code. ESP detailed guidelines are given in the ESP code.

ESP Code came into force on 01 January 2014.

ESP CODE.question: What is IACS and its role/function? what is ICS & IACS? Who all are the members of IACS?
ESP CODE.answer: IACS made - UR Z10 (hull survey requirements), applicable to bulk carriers, chemical tankers, oil tankers.(for all three types of ships) After these requirements , It was found that the accidents reduced drastically so IMO made it Mandatory. Later IMO, Adopted ESP code which  were based on survey requirements made by IACS into mandatory form only for oil tankers and bulk carriers. chemicla tankers doesnt come under ESP CODE

ESP CODE.question: ESP PROCESS?
ESP CODE.answer:
SURVEY PROGRAMME (what all things to be checked, made by company(ship owner) and class(RO) - done before 6 month before renewal survey in drydock - tells what all to be checked and what all areas) >SURVEY PLANNING MEETING/QUESTIONAIRE 
ship has to prepare
1. Particulars of ship
2. Ballasting and Deballasting 
3. Loading and Discharging
4.Ship Inspection Reports (SCF)
5. Paint Condition (CTF)
6. Anode Conditions (ICCP and MGPS logs)
7. Structural Design Documents
8. IG system Records
 all this will be sent to Class which is acting as RO for Administration.

ship send to owners office than to class



(depends on psc inspection, ism related nc /mnc, cargo carried, any damage occured) > CONDUCTING OF SURVEY (overall survey, closeup survey - check stringers, side shell plating's, bulkheads, inner structure) > SURVEY REPORTING > ESP FILE

ESP CODE.question: COATING CONDITION OR RATING when the survey is conducted?
ESP CODE.answer:
1. GOOD - condition with only minor spot rusting.
2. FAIR - condition with local breakdown of coating at edges of stiffeners and weld connections and/or light rusting over 20% or more of areas under consideration, but less than as defined for POOR Condition
3.POOR - condition with general breakdown of coating over 20% or more of areas or hard scale at 10% or more areas under consideration.

ESP CODE.question: Different Provisions and methods to access to structures?
ESP CODE.answer:
1. Permanent Staging
2. Temporary Staging
3. Rafts (old time - in tankers as hatch opening is small) but as presnt only staging is being done or drones are being used.
4. Portable Ladders
5.Direct Access
6. Cherry Picker (Bulk Carriers - as hatch opening is big)

ESP CODE.question: How to prepare for survey
ESP CODE.answer:
1. tanks and spaces shall be safe to access, tanks and spaces shall be gas free and properly ventilated.
2. Access to Structures - Permanent or temporary Staging, rafts, portable ladders, other equivalent means
3. Equipment for survey - certification of thickness measurement team is to be checked which is provided by administration also equipment's and its calibration certificate.
4. Enclosed Space Entry Procedures are to be followed - explosimeter, oxygen meter, breathing appratus, lifelines etc.

5.

Governor.question: What is governor?
Governor.answer:
1️⃣ Surveyor Accepted Answer

A governor is a device that automatically regulates the fuel supply to an engine to maintain constant speed, even when the load changes.

2️⃣ Core Insight with Detailed Example
Core Idea
Engines cannot maintain speed automatically under changing loads.

Story / Scenario (Hook)

Imagine you are sitting in the Engine Control Room (ECR) on a ship:

A pump starts suddenly → electrical load on generator increases.
Engine speed tries to drop because the same engine now has to deliver more power.
Without a governor: Speed drops → lights dim → pumps slow → engine unstable → voltage/frequency may fail.

Now, with a governor installed:

Governor senses speed drop immediately.
It moves the fuel rack to supply more fuel.
Engine power increases → speed restored → load is handled smoothly.

Key Insight: Governor doesn’t control the load; it reacts to load changes by controlling fuel, keeping engine speed steady.

Analogy (Everyday Life)
Cruise Control in a car:
Uphill → car slows → cruise control increases fuel → speed constant.
Downhill → car speeds → cruise control reduces fuel → speed constant.

Memory hook: Governor = Automatic Fuel Manager → Keeps Speed Constant


LOAD CHANGE ↑ or ↓
       ↓
ENGINE SPEED TENDS TO CHANGE
       ↓
GOVERNOR SENSES SPEED VARIATION
       ↓
FUEL RACK ADJUSTS ↑ or ↓
       ↓
ENGINE POWER INCREASES or DECREASES
       ↓
ENGINE SPEED RESTORED TO NORMAL
       ↓
LOAD HANDLED STABLY

5️⃣ Surveyor Accepted Answer (Reinforced)
A governor is a device that automatically regulates fuel supply to an engine to maintain constant speed, by reacting to load changes and stabilizing engine operation.


| Mistake                                                 | Reality / Core Insight                                                   |
| ------------------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------ |
| “Governor controls load directly”                       | ❌ Load is external; governor **reacts to speed changes caused by load**  |
| “Engine speed is instantly restored”                    | ❌ There’s a **reaction time**, but it’s fast enough for stable operation |
| “All governors are same for single/parallel generators” | ❌ Types differ: **Droop vs Isochronous**; parallel needs droop           |
| “Only controls speed, not power”                        | ❌ By controlling fuel → power changes → speed maintained                 |

Governor.question: Difference between M/E & A/E Governor ?
Governor.answer:
1️⃣ Surveyor Accepted Answer

ME Governor (Main Engine Governor): Controls the fuel supply to the main engine to maintain required engine speed under varying loads.
AE Governor (Auxiliary Engine Governor): Controls the fuel supply to auxiliary engines/generators to maintain constant speed and frequency for electrical load demands.

2️⃣ Insight with Elaborated Example
Core Idea / Why They Exist
ME Governor:
Main engine drives the ship’s propeller → speed must match navigation requirements.
Load on the main engine changes constantly (ship going uphill, turning, maneuvering).
ME governor maintains engine speed, ensuring smooth propulsion.
AE Governor:
Auxiliary engines power the ship’s electrical system (lights, pumps, electronics).
Electrical loads fluctuate constantly → voltage and frequency must stay stable.
AE governor maintains exact engine speed, so frequency of generators remains constant.
Scenario – Engine Room Example
Main Engine (ME) Example:
Ship accelerating → load increases → ME governor increases fuel → maintains engine RPM → smooth propulsion.
Auxiliary Engine (AE) Example:
Ship’s pumps and lights suddenly switch on → load on generator rises → AE governor senses speed drop → fuel adjusted → RPM restored → voltage/frequency stable.

Memory hook:

ME = Ship movement (propulsion speed)
AE = Electrical power (frequency/voltage)
3️⃣ Flowchart
ME Governor (Main Engine)
LOAD CHANGE (propulsion demand ↑/↓)
          ↓
ENGINE SPEED TENDS TO CHANGE
          ↓
ME GOVERNOR ADJUSTS FUEL
          ↓
MAIN ENGINE SPEED STABILIZED
          ↓
SHIP PROPULSION SMOOTH
AE Governor (Auxiliary Engine)
LOAD CHANGE (electrical demand ↑/↓)
          ↓
ENGINE SPEED TENDS TO CHANGE
          ↓
AE GOVERNOR ADJUSTS FUEL
          ↓
AUX ENGINE SPEED RESTORED
          ↓
VOLTAGE & FREQUENCY STABLE


4️⃣ Trap Insight (Common Mistakes)
Mistake	Reality / Insight
“ME and AE governors are the same”	❌ ME controls main engine speed; AE controls auxiliary engine speed for electrical load
“AE governor controls load”	❌ AE governor reacts to speed drop caused by load, does not control load directly
“ME governor maintains electrical frequency”	❌ Only AE governor maintains generator frequency

Exam Tip: Think ME → propulsion RPM, AE → generator RPM/frequency

5️⃣ Surveyor Accepted Answer (Reinforced)

ME Governor (Main Engine Governor): Controls the fuel supply to the main engine to maintain required engine speed under varying loads.
AE Governor (Auxiliary Engine Governor): Controls the fuel supply to auxiliary engines/generators to maintain constant speed and frequency for electrical loads.

What is Isochronous and Droop Governor?
Governor.answer:
Onboard ⚡:

Some systems need exact constant speed
Some systems need load sharing stability

👉 So we have two modes:

Isochronous → No speed change (during load variation)
Droop → Small speed change allowed (during load variation)


🔹 2. Real Ship Example
⚡ Isochronous (Single Generator)
Only one generator running
Load changes (pump, crane)
RPM must remain constant
👉 Frequency stays stable

⚡ Droop (Parallel Generators)
Two generators running together
Load shared between them
👉 Small speed drop ensures smooth sharing
🔹 3. Logic Flow
Load ↑ → Speed ↓
Isochronous → Corrects fully (no drop)
Droop → Allows small drop

          ⚙️ Load Increase
                │
      ┌─────────┴─────────┐
      │                   │
      ▼                   ▼
Isochronous         Droop Governor
      │                   │
Speed Restored       Speed Drops Slightly
Exactly              (Intentional)
      │                   │
❌ Not Stable         ✅ Stable Load
in Parallel           Sharing


Iso = Same speed always
Droop = Drop in speed

✅ 1. Surveyor Accepted Answer
“An isochronous governor maintains constant engine speed irrespective of load variations.”

1️⃣ Surveyor Accepted Answer

Droop Governor: Allows a small drop in engine speed as load increases, enabling parallel generators to share load safely.
Isochronous Governor: Maintains constant engine speed, used when a single generator runs alone, ensuring stable frequency for electrical loads (AE generators).

🔹 1. Real Onboard Situation

You have:

Generator 1 (Running)
Generator 2 (Standby)

Both are set in Isochronous mode

🔹 2. Step-by-Step Scenario (Very Practical)
⚡ Step 1: Start Generator 1
Running alone
Speed constant
Frequency stable

👉 No problem ✔

⚡ Step 2: Start Generator 2
Same RPM adjusted
Same voltage
Ready for parallel

👉 Still okay ✔

⚡ Step 3: Synchronizing
Match:
Voltage
Frequency
Phase

👉 Close breaker

👉 Now BOTH generators connected

⚠️ Step 4: Load Sharing Begins (Problem Starts)

Now:

Load increases slightly (pump starts)

👉 Speed tends to drop slightly

⚠️ What Generator 1 does:
“Speed dropped → I must correct fully”
👉 Increases fuel aggressively
⚠️ What Generator 2 does:
“Speed dropped → I must also correct fully”
👉 Also increases fuel aggressively
❗ RESULT

👉 Both are trying to take FULL load

➡️ No coordination
➡️ No sharing

🔥 What Happens Practically:
Load oscillates between generators
One takes more load → then other takes
Frequency fluctuates
System becomes unstable

👉 This is called:
⚠️ Hunting / Load Swinging

👉 In worst case:
❌ Generator trips
❌ Blackout

GOVERNORS.question: isochronous governor where it is used ?

GOVERNORS.answer:
Ans: Sir, according to Construction, there are 3 types of Governors:

Also, according to operation it can be classified into 2 types:

GOVERNORS.question: What is Governor? What is its function?
GOVERNORS.answer:
👉 “Governor controls fuel supply as per load.”
👉 “It sends signal to fuel pump → controls fuel rack movement.”
👉 “By controlling fuel → it controls speed, load, or both (within a range).”


❌ Governor does NOT directly control:

Speed
Load

👉 ✅ It controls:



Fuel rack position → fuel quantity → power → speed/load


“Governor controls fuel rack position based on load changes to maintain engine speed and load within a set range.”

⬇️ Less Fuel           ⬆️ More Fuel
(Overspeed)           (Load ↑ / Speed ↓)
        ↓
🔥 Engine Power Changes
